title: ThingWorx Services & SQL MCQ Quiz – Code Focus
description: 30 MCQs on ThingWorx services, SQL, built-in code snippets, and CRUD. 20 basic, 5 intermediate, 5 advanced; at least 20 are code-based.

questions.question: In a ThingWorx JavaScript service, what does this line do?
options: Reads the Temperature property from an unrelated Thing
Reads the Temperature property from the current Thing where the service is defined
Declares a new local variable named me
Invokes a remote service on another server
questions.answerText: Reads the Temperature property from the current Thing where the service is defined
questions.explanation: Inside a ThingWorx JavaScript service, 'me' refers to the current Thing/Template/Shape, so 'me.Temperature' reads its Temperature property.
questions.codeSnippet:
// JavaScript service code
var temp = me.Temperature;

questions.question: Given the following service, what is the new value of Counter after one execution when it initially equals 10?
options: Counter stays at 10
Counter becomes 11
Counter becomes 20
Counter becomes 0
questions.answerText: Counter becomes 11
questions.explanation: The service increments the property by 1, so a starting value of 10 becomes 11.
questions.codeSnippet:
// JavaScript service on a Thing
me.Counter = me.Counter + 1;

questions.question: This code uses a built-in Resource. What is created by the call to CreateInfoTableFromDataShape?
options: A Mashup definition for a table widget
An empty INFOTABLE that follows the specified Data Shape
A new Data Table entity in Composer
A SQL table in the persistence provider
questions.answerText: An empty INFOTABLE that follows the specified Data Shape
questions.explanation: CreateInfoTableFromDataShape returns an INFOTABLE instance whose columns are defined by the given Data Shape.

questions.question: In this snippet, what does values.AddRow(...) do?
options: Adds a new property to the Thing
Adds a new row to the INFOTABLE values using the provided field values
Deletes an existing Data Table row
Creates a new Mashup for the table
questions.answerText: Adds a new row to the INFOTABLE values using the provided field values
questions.explanation: AddRow inserts a new row into the INFOTABLE with the given field values matching the Data Shape's columns.

questions.question: What does the following line do when executed in a service?
options: Queries all rows in the Data Table
Deletes one row from the Data Table using its primary key
Deletes every row in the Data Table
Creates a new Data Table entity named MyTable
questions.answerText: Deletes one row from the Data Table using its primary key
questions.explanation: DeleteDataTableEntry removes a single entry whose key matches the keyvalue parameter.
questions.codeSnippet: Things['MyTable'].DeleteDataTableEntry({ keyvalue: 'ROW-001' });

questions.question: Which CRUD operation is being performed in this snippet?
options: Create
Read
Update
Delete
questions.answerText: Create
questions.explanation: AddDataTableEntry inserts a new row (create operation) into the Data Table.

questions.question: In a Database Thing service of type SQL Query, which placeholder correctly references an input parameter named deviceId?
options: ${deviceId}
[[deviceId]]
::deviceId::
{{deviceId}}
questions.answerText: [[deviceId]]
questions.explanation: Database Thing SQL services use the [[name]] syntax to reference input parameters inside the SQL text.
questions.codeSnippet: SELECT * FROM readings WHERE device_id = [[deviceId]];

questions.question: Given this SQL Query service on a Database Thing, what will it return?
options: A BOOLEAN indicating success only
An INFOTABLE containing all columns and rows from the 'sensors' table
The number of affected rows
No result because SQL Query does not return data
questions.answerText: An INFOTABLE containing all columns and rows from the 'sensors' table
questions.explanation: SQL Query services return rows as an INFOTABLE when executing SELECT statements.
questions.codeSnippet:
// SQL Query body
SELECT * FROM sensors;

questions.question: This snippet comes from a built-in Try/Catch code template. What does the catch block achieve?
options: It retries the service automatically
It logs the error and rethrows it so the caller can handle the failure
It silently ignores the error
It converts the error to a Data Table row
questions.answerText: It logs the error and rethrows it so the caller can handle the failure
questions.explanation: The typical ThingWorx snippet logs the error with logger.error and then throws it again to signal failure to callers.

questions.question: What will be logged by the following code when value is 5?
options: The logger prints nothing
The logger prints 'Value is: 5' at INFO level
The logger prints 'Value is: 5' at ERROR level
The logger throws an exception because value is not a string
questions.answerText: The logger prints 'Value is: 5' at INFO level
questions.explanation: logger.info writes a message with INFO severity; JavaScript concatenation converts 5 to a string.
questions.codeSnippet:
var value = 5;
logger.info('Value is: ' + value);

questions.question: This code calls QueryDataTableEntries without a filter. What is the result?
options: A single row is returned
All rows in the Data Table are returned as an INFOTABLE
No rows are returned
Only rows with status = 'OK' are returned
questions.answerText: All rows in the Data Table are returned as an INFOTABLE
questions.explanation: Calling QueryDataTableEntries with an empty query object returns all entries.
questions.codeSnippet: var result = Things['MyTable'].QueryDataTableEntries({ query: { filters: {} } });

questions.question: What does this service return?
options: A STRING with the greeting only
An INFOTABLE with a single column and row
A NUMBER containing the length of the name
A Data Table entity
questions.answerText: A STRING with the greeting only
questions.explanation: The service concatenates 'Hello ' with the input name and returns that string.

questions.question: In this SQL Command service, which operation is being performed?
options: SELECT
INSERT
UPDATE
DELETE
questions.answerText: UPDATE
questions.explanation: The SQL statement uses UPDATE ... SET ... WHERE, which updates existing rows.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the WHERE clause in this SQL snippet?
options: It defines the table name
It restricts the update to rows whose id matches the input parameter
It sorts the rows by id
It creates a new index on id
questions.answerText: It restricts the update to rows whose id matches the input parameter
questions.explanation: The WHERE id = [[deviceId]] condition ensures only the targeted row is updated.
questions.codeSnippet: UPDATE devices SET enabled = 1 WHERE id = [[deviceId]];

questions.question: What is the effect of the for loop in this code?
options: It iterates over each row in the INFOTABLE named rows
It iterates over each property of the Thing
It iterates over each user in the system
It iterates over each Data Table entity
questions.answerText: It iterates over each row in the INFOTABLE named rows
questions.explanation: rows.getRowCount() returns the number of rows, and rows.rows[i] accesses each row.

questions.question: Assuming 'alarms' is an INFOTABLE, what does alarms.toJSON() return?
options: A STRING containing a JSON representation of the INFOTABLE
A Data Table entity
An XML document
A BOOLEAN indicating success
questions.answerText: A STRING containing a JSON representation of the INFOTABLE
questions.explanation: toJSON() serializes the INFOTABLE into a JSON string that can be logged or returned.
questions.codeSnippet:
var json = alarms.toJSON();
logger.debug(json);

questions.question: This snippet uses a built-in code snippet to create an INFOTABLE and add a single row. Which field value will appear in the first row's 'status' column?
options: 'PENDING'
'DONE'
'ERROR'
An empty string
questions.answerText: 'DONE'
questions.explanation: The AddRow call sets status: 'DONE', so that is the value stored for that column.

questions.question: What will this code return if rows is an INFOTABLE with three rows and the field 'value' equals 1, 2, and 3?
options: The average, which is 2
questions.explanation: The service sums 1 + 2 + 3 and returns 6.

questions.question: In this SQL snippet, what does ORDER BY timestamp DESC ensure?
options: Rows are inserted in descending order
Rows are returned sorted by timestamp from newest to oldest
Rows are grouped by timestamp
Rows are deleted based on timestamp
questions.answerText: Rows are returned sorted by timestamp from newest to oldest
questions.explanation: ORDER BY timestamp DESC sorts the result set with the most recent timestamps first.
questions.codeSnippet: SELECT * FROM readings ORDER BY timestamp DESC;

questions.question: What does LIMIT 10 do in this SQL Query service?
options: Limits execution time to 10 seconds
Returns only the first 10 rows of the result set
Creates 10 new rows
Deletes 10 rows from the table
questions.answerText: Returns only the first 10 rows of the result set
questions.explanation: LIMIT 10 restricts the number of returned rows to 10, which is common in report services.
questions.codeSnippet: SELECT * FROM alarms ORDER BY createdAt DESC LIMIT 10;

questions.question: What does this upsert pattern do when executed?
options: It always deletes the row first
It adds a new row if the key does not exist or updates the existing row if the key already exists
It only reads data from the Data Table
It purges all rows from the Data Table
questions.answerText: It adds a new row if the key does not exist or updates the existing row if the key already exists
questions.explanation: AddOrUpdateDataTableEntry implements an upsert operation: insert or update based on the primary key.

questions.question: Which rows does this QueryDataTableEntries call return?
options: All rows regardless of status
Only rows where status equals 'ACTIVE'
Only rows where status does not equal 'ACTIVE'
Only rows where status is null
questions.answerText: Only rows where status equals 'ACTIVE'
questions.explanation: The EQ filter on fieldName 'status' with value 'ACTIVE' restricts the result to active rows.

questions.question: What is the effect of this InfoTableFunctions.Query call?
options: It deletes matching rows from the INFOTABLE
It filters the 'rows' INFOTABLE using the specified QUERY object and returns a new INFOTABLE
It writes rows into a Data Table
It converts the INFOTABLE to JSON
questions.answerText: It filters the 'rows' INFOTABLE using the specified QUERY object and returns a new INFOTABLE
questions.explanation: InfoTableFunctions.Query applies the QUERY structure to an in-memory INFOTABLE and returns only matching rows.

questions.question: Assuming a PostgreSQL database, what does this SQL Query service return?
options: Only rows older than 1 day
Rows whose timestamp is within the last 1 day for the given device
All rows for all devices
An error because interval syntax is invalid
questions.answerText: Rows whose timestamp is within the last 1 day for the given device
questions.explanation: The WHERE clause filters by device_id and timestamp > NOW() - interval '1 day', giving the last 24 hours of history for that device.
questions.codeSnippet:
SELECT *
FROM readings
WHERE device_id = [[deviceId]]
  AND timestamp > NOW() - interval '1 day';

questions.question: What is a good reason to structure a service like this, with a separate helper that formats an INFOTABLE for a Mashup?
options: It prevents the service from being called by other Things
It cleanly separates data retrieval from presentation formatting so different Mashups can reuse the same core data
It forces all calls to go through SQL
It disables caching
questions.answerText: It cleanly separates data retrieval from presentation formatting so different Mashups can reuse the same core data
questions.explanation: Splitting concerns between core data services and formatting helpers improves reuse and maintainability.

questions.question: In ThingWorx 10.x, how can you pause execution inside a long JavaScript service to inspect local variables?
options: Insert a 'throw new Error()' statement
Use the built-in JavaScript debugger and set a breakpoint on the desired line
Log variables with logger.debug instead of pausing
Enable SQL trace on the Database Thing
questions.answerText: Use the built-in JavaScript debugger and set a breakpoint on the desired line
questions.explanation: The new debugger lets you attach to a running service, set breakpoints, and inspect variables without changing code.

questions.question: Which ScriptServices call can you use in a DevOps pipeline to validate that a JavaScript body is syntactically correct before deploying it?
options: ScriptServices.CheckScriptWithLinesAndColumns
ScriptServices.ExecuteImmediate
ScriptServices.DeleteAllScripts
ScriptServices.ExportMashup
questions.answerText: ScriptServices.CheckScriptWithLinesAndColumns
questions.explanation: CheckScriptWithLinesAndColumns analyzes a script and returns syntax problems with precise locations, which is useful for automated validation.

questions.question: What is the purpose of this subscription code?
options: To recalculate KPIs only
To audit property changes by writing before-and-after values into an Audit Data Table whenever a property changes
To delete audit entries automatically
To disable logging on the property
questions.answerText: To audit property changes by writing before-and-after values into an Audit Data Table whenever a property changes
questions.explanation: The subscription listens to DataChange events and records user, timestamps, and values into an Audit table.

questions.question: In this advanced SQL Query service, which best describes the result?
options: A list of devices with no telemetry
A joined result of devices and their latest reading time for the specified tenant
Only telemetry rows without devices
A list of tenants without any devices
questions.answerText: A joined result of devices and their latest reading time for the specified tenant
questions.explanation: The query joins devices to readings and groups by device id to find the latest timestamp per device for that tenant.
questions.codeSnippet:
SELECT d.id, d.name, MAX(r.timestamp) AS last_reading
FROM devices d
JOIN readings r ON r.device_id = d.id
WHERE d.tenant_id = [[tenantId]]
GROUP BY d.id, d.name;

questions.question: Why is this pattern of having a small coordinator service that calls multiple helper services recommended in complex ThingWorx solutions?
options: It reduces the number of Things in the model
It makes each helper service easier to test, reuse, and debug while keeping orchestration logic clear
It disables the debugger on helper services
It avoids the need for Data Tables entirely
questions.answerText: It makes each helper service easier to test, reuse, and debug while keeping orchestration logic clear
questions.explanation: Decomposing responsibilities into small services improves maintainability, testability, and separation of concerns.
